interview_name: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)

main_summary.summary: Joint oral history interview with sisters Dorie Ann Ladner and Dr. Joyce Ann Ladner, recorded September 22, 2011 at the Library of Congress by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program. Raised in Palmers Crossing outside Hattiesburg, Mississippi, the sisters traced their movement work from teenage NAACP youth-chapter activity with Medgar Evers, Clyde Kennard, Vernon Dahmer, and C.E. Clack through Jackson State expulsion, SNCC organizing in Natchez and the Mississippi Delta, the August 1963 March on Washington (where Joyce worked in Bayard Rustin's Harlem office and Dorie organized for SNCC), the founding of COFO in Clarksdale in August 1962, the Tougaloo Nine support demonstrations, the 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer and the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge in Atlantic City, and the murders of Medgar Evers, Mack Charles Parker, Emmett Till, James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, Michael Schwerner, and Vernon Dahmer. Both describe the formation of a maternal-line ethos of dignity traced to Creek Indian ancestor Mary and white North Carolinian Benjamin Willard, and the Emmett Till generation's conviction that change required their action.
key_themes: family ethos and Emmett Till generation
main_summary.historical_significance:
This joint interview is a primary-source record of the SNCC Mississippi project from two participants who were on the front line of voter-registration, COFO founding, Freedom Summer, the MFDP Atlantic City challenge, and the response to the murders of Medgar Evers (June 1963), the Schwerner-Chaney-Goodman case (June 1964), and Vernon Dahmer (January 1966). The sisters' first-hand testimony from inside the March on Washington staging area-including the dispute over John Lewis's speech text and Lena Horne yielding her television interview to a 19-year-old Joyce on the Lincoln Memorial platform-documents details rarely recorded elsewhere.

The interview also preserves the maternal-line genealogy linking Mississippi Black activist organizing to a Creek Indian woman, Mary, and a white North Carolinian, Benjamin F. Willard, born 1805 in Rockingham, North Carolina. The sisters' explanation that "Grandpa Ben said we're as good as anybody" as a five-generation family mantra documents one route by which post-Reconstruction Black Southern families transmitted resistance to Jim Crow.

chapters.title: March on Washington 1963
chapters.summary: Dorie and Joyce describe their work for the August 1963 March on Washington in the weeks after Medgar Evers's June 12 assassination. Joyce was recruited by Ruby Doris Smith Robinson to work in Bayard Rustin's Harlem office; Dorie worked in the downtown SNCC office for Rachelle Horowitz and Eleanor Holmes. The sisters recount staying at the Statler-Hilton hotel, encountering Lena Horne, Josephine Baker, and Bob Dylan, and the on-platform dispute over John Lewis's speech text.

chapters.title: Birmingham Bombing Aftermath
chapters.summary: Three weeks after the March, Bob Moses drove a school bus of SNCC workers to the Birmingham funeral of the four girls killed in the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing. Dorie describes the stress-induced stomach illness she developed working in Natchez, the rage of running up to white police officers at the Masonic Temple after Medgar Evers's killing, and the realization decades later that the officers had practiced non-violence with her.

chapters.title: Byron De La Beckwith Trials
chapters.summary: Dorie attended both Beckwith trials in Jackson and watched Ross Barnett walk into court and shake the defendant's hand. She recounts how the Klan rose and gave Beckwith a standing ovation, how district attorney William Waller addressed the all-white jury with overt racism, and how Judge ejected her from the courtroom. The sisters and Dave Dennis and Mattie Bivens escaped a mob by riding away in a Volkswagen bus offered by a passerby.
related_events: Byron De La Beckwith first trial

chapters.title: Palmers Crossing Childhood
chapters.summary: The sisters describe their Hattiesburg-area home in Palmers Crossing: three bedrooms, a front porch, no fences between their house and their grandparents' and great aunt and uncle's homes. They recount Mr. Joe McCarthy's bootleg-whiskey operation, Miss Bama's brothel across the street, the Mississippi National Guard raid one Friday evening, and the Hudson family grocery store that grew into Hudson's salvage stores.
related_events: Mississippi National Guard raid on bootleg operation

chapters.title: Family Ethos Five Generations
chapters.summary: The sisters trace their family's resistance ethos to Benjamin F. Willard, born 1805 in Rockingham, North Carolina, and Mary, described as a Black Creek Indian, who came together to the Alabama-Mississippi territory and had thirteen children. Willard would not enslave his own children. The maternal-line mantra "we're as good as anybody" was passed through five generations. Their mother taught them to look white men in the eye when speaking.

chapters.title: Emmett Till Generation
chapters.summary: Joyce describes coining the term "Emmett Till generation" for post-war Black Southern teenagers whose parents, aunts, and uncles told them they would change things. They were the first generation often to go to college. The sisters describe clipping articles about the Emmett Till case from the Hattiesburg American at age 12, Joyce showing the scrapbook to her teenage "play mama" Modell Stewart, and the iconic Jet Magazine photograph that galvanized Black teenagers across the country.

chapters.title: Hattiesburg NAACP Youth and Mack Charles Parker
chapters.summary: The sisters met Medgar Evers in high school when he came to Hattiesburg to set up the NAACP youth chapter. Their chapter advisors were Clyde Kennard and Vernon Dahmer. Dahmer's sister Hallean Beard, the church pianist, was Dorie's best friend and chaperoned them to Jackson NAACP meetings. They describe Kennard's frame-up jailing on a moonshine charge after attempting to enroll at Mississippi Southern, and the 1959 lynching of Mack Charles Parker in Poplarville, Mississippi, fifteen minutes from Hattiesburg.

chapters.title: Founding COFO in Clarksdale
chapters.summary: Dorie recounts the August 1962 founding of COFO in Clarksdale, Mississippi-her first time going to the Delta. She traveled with Bob Moses, Dave Dennis, Mattie Dennis, and others. After a midnight curfew violation, Dave Dennis was nearly arrested; the group spent the night driving in fear through Ruleville, Mississippi, where one of Emmett Till's killers worked as a night watchman, looking for Amzie Moore's house in Cleveland. The experience pushed Dorie to commit to Delta organizing alongside sharecroppers like Fannie Lou Hamer.
related_events: Fannie Lou Hamer voter registration August 1962

chapters.title: Jackson State Expulsion
chapters.summary: The sisters attended Jackson State, where James Meredith was their strategist before he enrolled at Ole Miss. They demonstrated in support of the Tougaloo Nine and were tear-gassed. Dorie, as dorm-council president, called on Joyce to pray at a meeting where Joyce spoke of "dark days ahead." The dorm matron reported the prayer to Dean Oscar Rogers. The sisters and student-government president Walter Williams were expelled. Dorie left college; Joyce continued, transferring to Tougaloo.
related_events: James Meredith Ole Miss enrollment September 1962

chapters.title: Freedom House and Freedom Riders
chapters.summary: Joyce describes the Jackson Freedom House on Rose Street, where Lawrence Guyot, Cole Ladell, and others lived and cooked. She first encountered the Freedom Riders at the Masonic Temple by Medgar Evers's office, meeting Tom Hayden, Charles McDew, Marion Barry, John Lewis, and Dion Diamond. She describes the Freedom Riders' arrival as catalytic, with Women Power Unlimited and the local community providing material support, and the Friday-night community-organizing classes that followed.

chapters.title: Victoria Gray and MFDP Atlantic City
chapters.summary: The sisters describe their cousin Victoria Gray, who worked first with SCLC's Citizenship Education Program and then joined SNCC, as a teacher and confidant. They explain the building of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party from precinct through district through state conventions, with Ella Baker as guiding mentor. Joyce recounts driving in the Sojourner Fleet of cars-named by Ruby Doris Smith Robinson-from Mississippi through Denmark, South Carolina to Atlantic City, where the burned-out station wagon from the Schwerner-Chaney-Goodman murders was displayed on the boardwalk.

chapters.title: Schwerner Chaney Goodman
chapters.summary: Dorie had seen Schwerner, Chaney, and Goodman at Oxford, Ohio just before they returned to Mississippi after the church-burning report. Joyce describes the moment the WATS-line check-in failed and the staff realized the three were dead. The sisters express their judgment that the federal government's failure to intervene cost the three their lives-cooks at the jail had told the community they had advance word.

chapters.title: Natchez Bombing and Greenwood
chapters.summary: Dorie recounts a Klan bomb attempt on the Natchez SNCC office that hit the wrong house, an integrated cafe belonging to Jake Fishman and his Black partner. She and Mr. Archie Curtis went into the office to retrieve voter-registration cards through a mob. She describes a Greenwood voter-registration day at the Leflore County Courthouse where police dogs chased the group and a young Marian Wright-then at Yale Law School-called John Doar at the Justice Department from a pay phone.

chapters.title: Vernon Dahmer and Walking Wounded
chapters.summary: Joyce describes Vernon Dahmer's January 1966 murder as her worst movement moment-a 6 a.m. phone call from her mother to her graduate-school apartment, after years of repression of trauma broke through. She reflects on the movement's "walking wounded," SNCC veterans who did not survive the violence intact. Joyce describes Mrs. Hartman Turnbow carrying a pistol in a brown paper bag during the Atlantic City lobbying meeting with Rep. Edith Green and Senator Wayne Morse.

chapters.title: Genealogy and Closing
chapters.summary: Dorie describes her 1992 entry into genealogy research after Mississippi opened its slave records. Through cross-referenced research she connected with white Willard descendants in Georgia, Alabama, and London, eventually meeting in Mobile, Alabama. The line traces from Benjamin F. Willard (born 1805, Rockingham, North Carolina) and Mary (Creek Indian) through Frank, Jerry Willard, Joseph Collins Willard and Martha Gates, to their mother (born 1921, died 1995). Joyce reflects that the schools must teach the history so the next generations know what happened.
related_events: Mississippi archives opened to Black researchers 1992

loc_video.caption: Dorie Ann Ladner and Joyce Ladner oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Washington, D.C., 2011 September 20
